concept.title: Elements, Attributes, and the XML Tree
concept.shortdesc: XML documents are built from elements and attributes that form a hierarchical tree structure — understanding this tree is the key to working with XML effectively.

section.title: Elements: The building blocks of XML
section.p: An element is the fundamental unit of XML. It consists of a start tag, content, and an end tag:
section.codeblock: <title>Learning XML</title> ↑ ↑ ↑ start tag content end tag

section.title: Element naming rules
section.p: XML element names must follow these rules:

ul.li: Must start with a letter or underscore (_)
Can contain letters, digits, hyphens, underscores, and periods
Cannot start with the letters xml (reserved)

section.codeblock:
<!-- VALID element names --> <firstName> <first-name> <first_name> <_internal>
<!-- INVALID element names --> <1stName> <first name> <xml-data>
section.note: Choose a consistent naming convention for your XML vocabulary. Common styles: camelCase, kebab-case, or snake_case. DITA uses kebab-case for file names and camelCase for some attribute values.

section.title: Attributes: Metadata on elements
section.p: Attributes provide additional information about an element. They appear inside the start tag as name-value pairs:

ul.li: Values must be quoted (single or double quotes)
An element cannot have duplicate attribute names
Attributes cannot contain child elements — they hold simple values only

section.title: When to use elements vs. attributes
section.p: A common design question: should data be an element or an attribute? Here are guidelines:

row.entry: The data is a simple value (ID, type, status)
The data qualifies or classifies the element
The data appears at most once per element
The data is the primary content
The data is metadata about the content

section.note: When in doubt, use elements. They are more flexible and can be extended later without breaking existing documents.

section.title: The XML document tree
section.p: Every XML document forms a tree (also called the document object model or DOM). Understanding the tree is essential for navigation using XPath and for understanding schema validation.
These terms — root, parent, child, sibling, descendant, ancestor — are the vocabulary you will use throughout XML, XPath, XSLT, and DITA.

section.title: A preview: XML Namespaces
section.p: When different XML vocabularies are combined in one document, names can collide. XML Namespaces solve this by prefixing element names with a URI-based identifier:
section.codeblock: <html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:svg="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"> <body> <svg:svg width="100" height="100"> <svg:circle cx="50" cy="50" r="40"/> </svg:svg> </body> </html>
section.p: You will encounter namespaces when working with XSD schemas (which use the xs: or xsd: prefix) and when combining multiple XML standards in one document.
